source: Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS), FAO price monitoring, market trader surveys
disclaimer: Seasonal trends are approximate and vary by year depending on weather patterns, government policy, and regional factors. Use as planning guidance, not guaranteed predictions.

seasonal_trends.peak_reason: Lean season — stored harvest depleted, demand exceeds supply before March planting season
seasonal_trends.low_reason: First harvest season — abundant fresh supply from main growing regions (Eastern, Northern Uganda)
seasonal_trends.business_tip: Buy maize in bulk during June-August harvest at UGX 90,000-100,000/bag, store in airtight containers with proper drying (moisture below 13%), sell in December-February at UGX 160,000-180,000/bag. Storage costs: tarpaulin and sisal bags UGX 20,000-30,000 per 10 bags. Profit potential: UGX 50,000-70,000 per bag after storage costs.

seasonal_trends.peak_reason: Dry season depletes stocks, high demand for school feeding programmes as Term 1 begins in February
seasonal_trends.low_reason: Main harvest season, especially in Western and Eastern Uganda
seasonal_trends.business_tip: Beans store well for 6+ months if properly dried and treated with ash or actellic dust to prevent weevils. Buy at harvest in July (UGX 250,000-280,000) and sell in February-March (UGX 400,000-450,000). Schools are bulk buyers — approach school bursars in January.

seasonal_trends.peak_reason: Heavy rains damage crops, reduce supply from major growing areas (Mbale, Kasese, Kapchorwa). Transport disrupted by flooded roads.
seasonal_trends.low_reason: Dry season harvest, abundant supply from irrigated farms and imports from Kenya
seasonal_trends.business_tip: Tomatoes are highly perishable — price swings create opportunities but also risks. Consider tomato paste/sauce production as a value-addition business to smooth out seasonal volatility. A simple tomato sauce operation needs: cooking pots, bottles, labels (startup UGX 300,000-500,000).

seasonal_trends.peak_reason: Local supply gap between harvest seasons, increased demand during Ramadan/Eid period
seasonal_trends.low_reason: Harvest from Kasese, Kapchorwa, and imported onions from Tanzania flood the market
seasonal_trends.business_tip: Onions store better than most vegetables — up to 3 months in a cool, dry, ventilated space. Buy in November-December at UGX 140,000-160,000 and sell in April-May at UGX 250,000-280,000. Storage losses (rotting, sprouting) average 10-15%, so factor that into your margins.

seasonal_trends.peak_reason: Peak construction season — builders rush to complete projects before rains. High demand drives prices up.
seasonal_trends.low_reason: Construction slows dramatically — wet ground makes foundation work impossible, many sites pause. Suppliers discount to move stock.
seasonal_trends.business_tip: If you are in construction or supply building materials, stock up on cement and sand during the rainy season (April-June) when prices drop 15-20%. Store cement off the ground on pallets, covered with tarpaulin. Hardware shops near construction zones should plan inventory around these cycles.

seasonal_trends.peak_reason: Dry season construction boom increases demand. Sand mining easier in dry conditions but demand outstrips supply.
seasonal_trends.low_reason: Rainy season reduces construction activity. However, flooding can also disrupt sand extraction from rivers.
seasonal_trends.business_tip: Sand and aggregate supply is a capital-intensive but profitable seasonal business. One truck trip from Lwera sand mines to Kampala costs UGX 200,000 in transport and sells for UGX 300,000-350,000. Establish reliable buyer relationships with contractors for consistent orders.

seasonal_trends.peak_reason: Festive season celebrations drive massive demand for chicken. Families, restaurants, and caterers all compete for supply.
seasonal_trends.low_reason: Post-festive lull, household budgets tight after holiday spending and school fees. Oversupply from farmers who missed festive deadlines.
seasonal_trends.business_tip: Time your poultry batches to mature 1 week before festive peaks. For Christmas: start broiler chicks in October (6-8 weeks to maturity). For Easter: count back 8 weeks from Easter date. For Eid: follow the Islamic calendar. Pre-sell to hotels and restaurants 2 weeks before the event. Festive pricing can double your profit per bird.

seasonal_trends.peak_reason: School feeding programmes create bulk demand. Also post-Christmas spending recovery — eggs are affordable protein.
seasonal_trends.low_reason: School holidays reduce institutional demand. Layer hens in peak production flood the market.
seasonal_trends.business_tip: Diversify your egg sales channels: direct to schools during term (negotiate supply contracts with school bursars), to hotels and restaurants year-round, and retail from your farm gate. Consistent supply builds customer loyalty. Consider egg preservation (water glass method) to sell stored eggs during peak-price periods.
